commonProblems.h2: Common Range Rover Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: Range Rover ownership brings an unmatched blend of luxury and capability, but certain engine families have developed well-documented, high-stakes failure patterns. The 3.0 SDV6/TDV6 diesel’s crankshaft failures and the 5.0 V8 supercharged petrol’s timing chain issues dominate replacement searches, while Ingenium 2.0 units and 4.4 TDV8 diesels present their own costly failures. Every reconditioned engine supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded internal components designed to address the original failure point — plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Below, we break down the most common failures, what they cost to fix at a dealer versus a specialist, and when replacement becomes the only sensible option.

problems.h4: 3.0 SDV6 / TDV6 Crankshaft Failure – The Catastrophic Weakness
problems.rootCause: Narrow main bearing journals and inadequate oil flow to the rear crankshaft web cause fatigue cracking. Crankshaft snaps without warning, often sending conrods through the block — instant engine death.
problems.recommendation: Once the crankshaft has snapped, there is no minor repair. The engine is scrap. For vehicles you plan to keep beyond two years, invest in a reconditioned SDV6 engine with uprated bearings. It costs more upfront but eliminates the factory flaw. Used engines carry the same design risk and often fail again within 40,000 miles. On a Range Rover worth £12,000–£25,000, a properly reconditioned engine gives you another 100,000 miles of life.
problems.cta: → Compare reconditioned 3.0 SDV6 engine prices for your L405 or L494 now

problems.h4: 5.0 V8 Supercharged Timing Chain Failure – The High‑Mileage Threat
problems.affectedModels: Range Rover (L322 2010-2012, L405 2013-2017), Range Rover Sport (L494 2014-2017, L320 2010-2013) — 5.0 V8 Supercharged (508PN)
problems.rootCause: Overly long 15,000-mile oil change intervals degrade the timing chain tensioners. Chains stretch, guides crack, and the engine jumps timing — bending valves and destroying pistons.
problems.recommendation: If your 5.0 V8 starts rattling on cold start-up and you catch it before the check engine light flashes, the timing chain replacement is the smarter financial move. Specialist pricing around £2,200-£3,200 is a fraction of replacement cost. However, if the engine has already jumped timing or metal debris is in the oil, go straight to a reconditioned unit with upgraded tensioners. Don’t throw good money after bad.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt 5.0 V8 supercharged engine prices with upgraded timing components

problems.h4: 4.4 TDV8 / SDV8 Crankshaft & Turbo Actuator Failures – The Long‑Distance Burden
problems.rootCause: Crankshaft fractures at the rear journal due to harmonic balancer fatigue. Simultaneously, secondary turbo actuator gears strip, causing ‘restricted performance’ mode. Coolant crossover pipes leak, leading to sudden overheating.
problems.recommendation: If you hear a knocking noise from the bottom end or the engine feels unbalanced, suspect crankshaft fracture immediately. Stop driving and fit a reconditioned unit. If your only symptoms are reduced power and a coolant leak, the actuator and pipe replacement is a cost-effective fix. But if the vehicle has covered over 110,000 miles and you plan to tow or cover long distances, pre-emptively replacing the engine with a reconditioned SDV8 unit gives you peace of mind for years.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for reconditioned 4.4 TDV8 engines with revised crankshafts

problems.h4: 2.0 Ingenium Diesel Timing Chain Stretch – The Out‑of‑Warranty Shock
problems.affectedModels: Range Rover Evoque (2015-2019), Range Rover Velar (2017-2020) — 2.0 Ingenium Diesel (204DTD, AJ200D)
problems.rootCause: Oil dilution from incomplete DPF regenerations degrades the timing chain and guides. Chains stretch, jump teeth, and the engine loses compression. Piston ring design contributes to oil contamination.
problems.recommendation: On a 2.0 Ingenium, timing chain rattle on cold start needs immediate attention. A preventative chain and guide replacement at a specialist for £1,500-£2,200 is significantly cheaper than a full rebuild. However, if the engine has already lost power and the check engine light is flashing for timing correlation faults, skip the rebuild and go straight to a reconditioned unit. On an Evoque or Velar worth £8,000-£15,000, a replacement engine with upgraded components gives you a like-new drivetrain for half the dealer’s replacement cost.
problems.cta: → Compare reconditioned 2.0 Ingenium engine prices for your Evoque or Velar

problems.h4: P400e Hybrid System & Engine Failures – The Emerging Problem
problems.affectedModels: Range Rover (L405 2019-2021, L460 2022+), Range Rover Sport (L494 2019-2022) — P400e 2.0 Plug-in Hybrid (PT204 petrol + electric motor)
problems.rootCause: Electric motor bearings fail, contaminating the engine oil with metal debris. The 2.0 Ingenium petrol engine suffers high-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failure, timing chain stretch, and coolant loss from the hybrid battery thermal management system.
problems.recommendation: P400e hybrid failures are the new frontier. If your Range Rover shows a hybrid system fault, unusual whining from the electric motor, or blackened engine oil, stop driving immediately. Do not attempt a minor HPFP or chain repair without having the motor and oil system inspected for metal debris. Once contaminated, the only safe route is a full replacement engine and motor package. Because fewer specialists handle hybrids, using EngineMarket to compare P400e-ready specialists will save you thousands over main dealer pricing.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for reconditioned P400e hybrid engine and motor packages

problems.h4: 2.7 TDV6 Oil Pump Housing Failure – The Silent Killer
problems.rootCause: Plastic oil pump housing fractures, causing sudden oil pressure loss. The engine seizes within seconds. No warning lights precede failure.
problems.recommendation: The 2.7 TDV6’s plastic oil pump housing is a ticking clock. If your Sport L320 is over 70,000 miles and the housing is original, spend £1,000 at a specialist to fit the upgraded aluminium version. This preventative fix saves the engine. However, if you’ve already experienced sudden oil pressure loss — usually accompanied by a bang and complete engine stop — the engine is seized. Only a reconditioned unit with the aluminium housing will give you reliable service.
problems.cta: → Compare reconditioned 2.7 TDV6 engine prices with upgraded oil pump housing

engineSizes.h2: Range Rover Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model
engineSizes.intro: Every Range Rover engine size explained — with the models and engine codes that used each displacement — so you can quickly identify the correct replacement unit for your vehicle.

items.title: Range Rover 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.0-litre diesel entered the Range Rover family with the Evoque and Velar as part of JLR’s Ingenium family. These engines replaced older Ford-derived units and prioritised efficiency, but timing chain stretch and oil dilution from DPF regenerations became widespread failure points. While fuel economy impresses, catastrophic chain failure often writes off vehicles out of warranty. Engine code(s): 204DTD, AJ200D Compatible models (UK): Range Rover Evoque (2015–2019), Range Rover Velar (2017–2020), Range Rover Sport (2018–2020 limited markets) Production years: 2015–present Common failure points: Timing chain stretch, oil dilution, DPF regeneration issues, high-pressure fuel pump failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Range Rover 2.0L diesel engines

items.title: Range Rover 2.2L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.2-litre diesel arrived via Ford’s Duratorq family, fitted almost exclusively to the first-generation Evoque. While more robust than early Ingenium units, these engines suffer from EGR valve clogging and turbocharger oil feed restrictions. Most replacement demand comes from high-mileage examples where the turbo has failed and sent debris through the engine. Engine code(s): 224DT (Ford Duratorq ZSD-422) Compatible models (UK): Range Rover Evoque (2011–2015) Production years: 2011–2015 Common failure points: EGR valve carbon buildup, turbo oil feed blockage, injector seal failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Range Rover 2.2L diesel engines

items.title: Range Rover 2.5L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.5-litre diesel belongs to the P38 generation, powered by BMW’s M51 straight-six. This is a classic engine – mechanically robust but now aged. Replacement demand comes from head gasket failure and injection pump seal leaks on high-mileage or poorly maintained examples. Few specialists still rebuild these, but used units remain available. Engine code(s): BMW M51D25 (2.5 TD) Compatible models (UK): Range Rover P38 (1996–2002) Production years: 1996–2002 Common failure points: Head gasket failure, injection pump seal leaks, cracked cylinder heads on overheated units
items.cta: Get quotes for Range Rover 2.5L diesel engines

items.title: Range Rover 2.7L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.7-litre diesel was the first TDV6, fitted to the early Sport L320 and the L322 full-size Range Rover. Ford and PSA co-developed this engine, but the plastic oil pump housing is its Achilles’ heel – when it fractures, oil pressure vanishes and the engine seizes without warning. Most replacement demand comes from sudden, catastrophic oil loss. Engine code(s): 276DT (Ford Lion V6) Compatible models (UK): Range Rover Sport L320 (2005–2009), Range Rover L322 (2006–2009 – limited) Production years: 2005–2009 Common failure points: Plastic oil pump housing fracture, crankshaft bearing wear, turbocharger failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Range Rover 2.7L diesel engines

items.title: Range Rover 3.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 3.0-litre diesel is the most common and most failure-prone engine in modern Range Rovers. The TDV6, SDV6, and later Ingenium six-cylinder diesels all share a devastating weakness: crankshaft bearing failure leading to a snapped crank. This is the single largest replacement driver across L322, L405, L494, and L460 models. Reconditioned units with uprated bearings are the only long-term fix. Engine code(s): 306DT (TDV6), 306DTX (SDV6 twin-turbo), AJ20D6 (Ingenium six-cylinder) Compatible models (UK): Range Rover L322 (2009–2012), L405 (2013–2021), Sport L320 (2009–2013), L494 (2014–2021), Velar (2017–present), L460 (2022–present) Production years: 2009–present Common failure points: Crankshaft bearing fatigue / snapped crankshaft, oil pump failure, turbo actuator seizure
items.cta: Get quotes for Range Rover 3.0L diesel engines

items.title: Range Rover 3.6L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 3.6-litre TDV8 was the first V8 diesel in a Range Rover, fitted exclusively to the L322. This Ford-Lion unit is smoother than the TDV6 but has its own demons: crankshaft bearing wear, turbo actuator failure, and EGR cooler leaks. Replacement demand comes mostly from high-mileage towing vehicles where the bottom end has fatigued. Engine code(s): 368DT (Ford Lion V8) Compatible models (UK): Range Rover L322 (2006–2010) Production years: 2006–2010 Common failure points: Crankshaft bearing wear, turbo actuator seizure, EGR cooler failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Range Rover 3.6L diesel engines

items.title: Range Rover 4.4L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 4.4-litre TDV8 and later SDV8 represent the pinnacle of Range Rover diesel engineering – but they are not immune to failure. Crankshaft fracture at the rear journal, secondary turbo actuator gear stripping, and coolant crossover pipe leaks are well-documented. Replacement is expensive, but reconditioned units with revised crankshafts and upgraded actuators offer a permanent fix. Engine code(s): 448DT (4.4 TDV8), 448DT (4.4 SDV8 – later revision) Compatible models (UK): Range Rover L322 (2010–2012), L405 (2013–2021), Sport L494 (2014–2020) Production years: 2010–2021 Common failure points: Crankshaft fracture, turbo actuator failure, coolant crossover pipe leaks, timing chain tensioner wear
items.cta: Get quotes for Range Rover 4.4L diesel engines

items.title: Range Rover 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.0-litre petrol arrived with the Ingenium family in the Evoque and Velar, later appearing as the combustion half of the P400e plug-in hybrid. These engines are prone to high-pressure fuel pump failure and timing chain stretch. Replacement demand is rising as these vehicles age out of warranty and main dealer quotes shock owners. Engine code(s): PT204, AJ200P Compatible models (UK): Range Rover Evoque (2015–present), Range Rover Velar (2017–present), Range Rover Sport L494 (2019–2022 – P400e hybrid) Production years: 2015–present Common failure points: High-pressure fuel pump failure, timing chain stretch, piston ring carbon buildup
items.cta: Compare Range Rover 2.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Range Rover 3.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 3.0-litre petrol – both supercharged V6 and later mild-hybrid inline-six – appears in higher-spec L405 and L494 models. The supercharged V6 suffers timing chain tensioner wear, while the later Ingenium petrol six is still proving its reliability. Replacement demand currently centres on earlier supercharged units where chain rattle has been ignored until catastrophic failure. Engine code(s): 306PS (3.0 V6 supercharged), AJ20P6 (3.0 Ingenium petrol mild hybrid) Compatible models (UK): Range Rover L405 (2013–2021), Range Rover Sport L494 (2014–2021) Production years: 2013–present Common failure points: Timing chain tensioner wear (supercharged V6), injector failure, coolant leaks
items.cta: Compare Range Rover 3.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Range Rover 4.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 4.0-litre Rover V8 belongs to the P38 generation – a pushrod, naturally aspirated engine that is simple by modern standards. Replacement demand comes from age-related failures: slipped cylinder liners, camshaft wear, and cracked distributor caps. Rebuilt units with top-hat liners solve the liner slip issue permanently. Engine code(s): Rover 40D (4.0 V8) Compatible models (UK): Range Rover P38 (1994–2002) Production years: 1994–2002 Common failure points: Slipped cylinder liners, camshaft wear, cooling system neglect
items.cta: Compare Range Rover 4.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Range Rover 4.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 4.2-litre supercharged V8 appeared in the Sport L320 and late L322 as a performance-oriented option. Based on the 4.4-litre naturally aspirated V8, the added supercharger stresses the timing chain guides and tensioners. Replacement demand comes from chain failure and supercharger coupler wear. Engine code(s): 428PS (4.2 V8 supercharged) Compatible models (UK): Range Rover Sport L320 (2005–2009), Range Rover L322 (2006–2009) Production years: 2005–2009 Common failure points: Timing chain guide failure, supercharger coupler wear, VANOS solenoid issues
items.cta: Compare Range Rover 4.2L petrol engine prices

items.title: Range Rover 4.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 4.4-litre naturally aspirated V8 – codenamed AJ41 – powered early L322 models and is widely considered one of the more reliable Range Rover petrol engines. Still, age takes its toll: VANOS units fail, timing chain guides become brittle, and valley pan gaskets leak. Replacement is uncommon, but when it happens, reconditioned units are the standard. Engine code(s): 448PN (M62B44 – BMW-derived AJ41) Compatible models (UK): Range Rover L322 (2002–2005 for M62; 2005–2009 for revised unit) Production years: 2002–2009 Common failure points: VANOS seal failure, timing chain guide brittleness, valley pan coolant leaks
items.cta: Compare Range Rover 4.4L petrol engine prices

items.title: Range Rover 4.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 4.6-litre Rover V8 was the larger displacement option in the P38 generation, offering more torque for towing. It shares the 4.0-litre’s Achilles’ heel: cylinder liner slippage when overheated. Replacement demand comes from owners who want to preserve a classic – rebuilt units with top-hat liners are the gold standard. Engine code(s): Rover 46D (4.6 V8) Compatible models (UK): Range Rover P38 (1994–2002) Production years: 1994–2002 Common failure points: Slipped cylinder liners, camshaft wear, distributor failure
items.cta: Compare Range Rover 4.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Range Rover 5.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 5.0-litre V8 – both naturally aspirated and supercharged – is the flagship Range Rover petrol engine. It delivers effortless power but suffers from timing chain tensioner wear and, on supercharged variants, coupler failure. Replacement demand is high among owners of L322, L405, and L494 models where chain rattle has been ignored until valve timing jumps. Reconditioned units with reinforced tensioners offer permanent peace of mind. Engine code(s): 508PN (5.0 V8 naturally aspirated), AJ133 (5.0 V8 supercharged / SVR) Compatible models (UK): Range Rover L322 (2010–2012), L405 (2013–2021), Sport L320 (2010–2013), L494 (2014–2021), Sport SVR (2015–2021) Production years: 2010–2021 Common failure points: Timing chain tensioner wear, supercharger coupler failure (supercharged), high-pressure fuel pump issues
items.cta: Compare Range Rover 5.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Range Rover 2.0L Hybrid Engines (P400e Plug-in Hybrid)
items.description: The P400e combines a 2.0-litre Ingenium petrol engine with a 105kW electric motor, fitted to L405, L494, and L460 models. Electric motor bearing failure can contaminate the engine oil with metal debris, while the hybrid battery thermal management system is prone to coolant leaks. Specialist diagnosis is essential – few general garages understand this system. System / motor code(s): PT204 petrol engine + unnamed 105kW rear-axle motor Compatible models (UK): Range Rover L405 (2019–2021), Range Rover Sport L494 (2019–2022), Range Rover L460 (2022–present) Production years: 2019–present Important notes: Motor bearing contamination can destroy both petrol engine and electric unit. Battery health check essential before any replacement.

items.title: Range Rover 3.0L MHEV (Mild Hybrid)
items.description: The 3.0-litre Ingenium petrol six-cylinder mild hybrid (MHEV) adds a belt-integrated starter generator (BiSG) to reduce emissions. While generally more reliable than plug-in hybrids, the BiSG unit can fail, and the complex 48V electrical system requires specialist diagnostic equipment. Replacement of the engine or BiSG component is rare but expensive when required. System / motor code(s): AJ20P6 (3.0 petrol) + BiSG unit Compatible models (UK): Range Rover L405 (2020–2021), Range Rover L460 (2022–present), Range Rover Sport L494 (2020–2022) Production years: 2020–present Important notes: 48V system requires high-voltage training. BiSG failure triggers repeated warning lights but rarely writes off the vehicle. Closing line: Not sure which engine size your Range Rover needs? items.title: Range Rover Diesel Engines
items.description: Short descriptor: Diesel has powered the majority of Range Rover models since the late 1990s, and today diesel engine replacement enquiries dominate the marketplace – particularly for the 3.0 SDV6 family with its well‑documented crankshaft failures. Common Range Rover Diesel Engine Families: 306DT / 306DTX (3.0 TDV6/SDV6) – L320, L322, L405, L494, L460 – crankshaft bearing fatigue is the #1 failure 276DT (2.7 TDV6) – L320 Sport, early L322 – items.title: Range Rover Hybrid & Electrified Engines
items.description: #### Range Rover Plug‑in Hybrid (P400e) Short descriptor: The P400e plug‑in hybrid pairs a 2.0‑litre Ingenium petrol engine with a 105kW electric motor, appearing on L405, L494, and L460 models – an emerging replacement market with specialist requirements. Common Range Rover P400e Components: PT204 (2.0 Ingenium petrol) – shared with non‑hybrid Evoque and Velar 105kW rear‑axle electric motor – integrated with the rear differential How Range Rover P400e Plug‑in Hybrid Systems Work: Electric‑only range of approximately 25‑30 miles on a full charge Petrol engine and electric motor can operate together for full power output Regenerative braking charges the high‑voltage battery during deceleration Found In: Range Rover L405 (2019‑2021) – P400e plug‑in hybrid Range Rover Sport L494 (2019‑2022) – P400e plug‑in hybrid Range Rover L460 (2022‑present) – P440e (updated hybrid system) Important Notes (Brand‑wide): Electric motor bearing failure can contaminate engine oil – catastrophic if ignored High‑voltage battery health deteriorates with age – expect 10‑15% capacity loss by 80,000 miles Only hybrid‑trained specialists should diagnose or replace P400e powertrains Engine replacement alone may not resolve hybrid system fault codes Reconditioned P400e packages (engine + motor) are available but require specialist sourcing #### Range Rover Mild Hybrid (MHEV) Short descriptor: The 3.0‑litre Ingenium petrol six‑cylinder mild hybrid adds a belt‑integrated starter generator (BiSG) and 48V electrical system, improving efficiency without plug‑in capability. Common Range Rover MHEV Components: AJ20P6 (3.0 Ingenium petrol) – inline six‑cylinder turbocharged BiSG (Belt‑integrated Starter Generator) – 48V motor/generator unit How Range Rover Mild Hybrid Systems Work: BiSG recovers energy during braking and coasting into a 48V battery Stored energy provides torque assistance under acceleration Allows engine stop‑start at higher speeds than conventional systems Found In: Range Rover L405 (2020‑2021) – 3.0 Ingenium petrol MHEV Range Rover L460 (2022‑present) – 3.0 Ingenium petrol MHEV (P360, P400) Range Rover Sport L494 (2020‑2022) – 3.0 Ingenium petrol MHEV Important Notes (Brand‑wide): BiSG unit failure triggers repeated warning lights but rarely writes off the engine 48V system requires diagnostic equipment most general garages do not possess Mild hybrid components are sealed and not user‑serviceable Engine replacement itself is similar to non‑hybrid petrol – the BiSG can be transferred or replaced separately Used engine availability for MHEV variants is still limited (2025 market)

entries.title: 306DT 3.0 TDV6 Diesel
entries.description: The 306DT powered the Range Rover Sport (L320, 2009–2013) and full-size Range Rover (L322, 2009–2012). Catastrophic crankshaft failure due to narrow bearing design is the dominant replacement driver, typically between 80,000 and 130,000 miles. A quality rebuilt unit includes uprated crankshaft bearings, a revised oil pump, and tested turbo actuators.

entries.title: 306DTX 3.0 SDV6 Diesel
entries.description: The twin‑turbo 306DTX is found in the Range Rover (L405, 2013–2017), Range Rover Sport (L494, 2013–2017), and Discovery 4/5. The same crankshaft bearing fatigue affects this higher‑output variant, often with little warning. Rebuilt SDV6 engines from our specialists include uprated bearings, revised oil pumps, and full turbo assembly testing.

entries.title: 276DT 2.7 TDV6 Diesel
entries.description: The 276DT was fitted to the Range Rover Sport (L320, 2005–2009) and Discovery 3. While less failure‑prone than the 3.0‑litre version, crankshaft bearing wear and oil pump housing failure still drive replacement, typically from 100,000 miles. Rebuilt units include upgraded bearings and a revised oil pump assembly.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 276DT Range Rover engine replacement

entries.title: 204DTD 2.0 Ingenium Diesel (150–180 HP)
entries.description: The 204DTD is used in the Range Rover Evoque (2015–2020), Velar (2017–present), and Discovery Sport. Timing chain stretch caused by oil dilution from incomplete DPF regenerations is the primary failure, often between 50,000 and 90,000 miles. Rebuilt units include upgraded timing chain kits, revised oil pumps, and thoroughly cleaned DPF systems.

entries.title: 204DTA 2.0 Ingenium Diesel (240 HP SD4/D240)
entries.description: The higher‑output 204DTA appears in the Evoque (2017–2020), Velar (2017–present), and Range Rover Sport (2016–2020). The same timing chain stretch and oil dilution issues affect this variant. A specialist rebuilt engine includes upgraded timing components, revised oil pumps, and full DPF system diagnostics.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 204DTA Range Rover engine replacement

entries.title: 508PN 5.0 V8 Supercharged Petrol
entries.description: The 508PN powers the full-size Range Rover (L322, L405) and Range Rover Sport (L494, L320). Timing chain tensioner wear due to 10,000‑mile oil change intervals is the dominant failure, typically between 70,000 and 110,000 miles. Rebuilt units include reinforced tensioners, revised oil pumps, and a new supercharger coupler.

entries.title: AJ133 5.0 V8 Supercharged (High Output / SVR)
entries.description: The AJ133 high‑output variant is exclusive to the Range Rover Sport SVR and Autobiography models. In addition to timing chain tensioner failure, supercharger coupler degradation is common. Rebuilt AJ133 engines include reinforced timing components, upgraded supercharger couplers, and bench testing to factory power specifications.

entries.title: 448PN 4.4 V8 Naturally Aspirated Petrol
entries.description: The 448PN (AJ41) was fitted to the L322 (2002–2009). This is a generally reliable unit, but VANOS issues and timing chain guide wear occur from 100,000+ miles. Rebuilt units include revised timing chain guides and VANOS service components.

entries.title: 448DT 4.4 SDV8 Diesel
entries.description: The 448DT is found in the Range Rover (L405, 2012–2017) and Range Rover Sport (L494, 2014–2017). Crankshaft fracture, coolant crossover pipe leaks, and turbo actuator faults drive replacement, typically from 90,000 miles. Rebuilt units include uprated crankshaft bearings, aluminium coolant pipes, and revised turbo actuators.

entries.title: 368DT 3.6 TDV8 Diesel
entries.description: The 368DT powered the L322 (2006–2010). Crankshaft bearing fatigue and turbo actuator failure are the primary replacement drivers, typically between 90,000 and 140,000 miles. Rebuilt units include upgraded bearings, revised turbo actuators, and new EGR components.

entries.title: 306DTA 3.0 Ingenium Diesel (D250/D300)
entries.description: The 306DTA is used in the Range Rover (L405, late 2018–2022) and Range Rover Sport (L494, 2018–2022). Bearing wear and timing chain stretch are emerging failure points as these units age. Rebuilt engines from our network include upgraded timing chain kits and revised bearing shells.

entries.title: P400e (PT204 + Electric Motor) 2.0 Plug‑in Hybrid
entries.description: The P400e hybrid combines the PT204 2.0 Ingenium petrol engine with an 85kW electric motor. Fitted to the Range Rover (L405, 2018–2022; L460, 2022–present) and Range Rover Sport (L494, 2018–2022). Timing chain failure on the petrol component and hybrid system controller faults drive specialist replacement demand. Rebuilt hybrid engines include upgraded timing components and full hybrid system diagnostics.

entries.title: 306D1 (M51) 2.5 TD Diesel
entries.description: The BMW M51 2.5‑litre turbo diesel powered the P38 Range Rover (1996–2002). Injection pump failure and turbo issues are the primary replacement drivers, typically after 100,000–150,000 miles. Rebuilt units include refurbished injection pumps and revised turbo oil feed lines.

years.preview: P38a Era: BMW Diesel & Rover V8 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The second-generation Range Rover (P38a) marked the brand's first use of a BMW-sourced diesel unit alongside a heavily revised Rover V8, establishing a template of mixed-supplier powertrain strategy that would persist for two decades. Key Changes BMW 2.5-litre six-cylinder diesel (256T) replaced the ageing VM diesel, bringing genuine refinement to Range Rover diesel buyers Rover 4.0 and 4.6-litre all-aluminium V8s replaced the carburettor-fed 3.9, gaining fuel injection across the range Introduction of GEMS (General Engine Management System) on petrol V8 variants, improving cold-start reliability Land Rover's first electronic engine management integrated with transmission control Main Engines 256T (BMW M51) - 2.5-litre six-cylinder diesel (Range Rover P38a) 14CUX/GEMS Rover V8 - 4.0 and 4.6-litre petrol (Range Rover P38a) Popular Models Range Rover P38a 2.5 DSE Range Rover P38a 4.0 SE Range Rover P38a 4.6 HSE Known For BMW 2.5 diesel offers excellent refinement but head gasket failures are well-documented above 100,000 miles Rover V8 aluminium block is lightweight and free-revving but prone to coolant loss and liner seal failure at high mileage GEMS-managed V8s respond well to specialist rebuilds; parts supply remains strong via enthusiast network P38a diesel buyers frequently report injector return-pipe degradation causing rough running and hard starting [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: 256T, 14CUX, GEMS V8 4.0, GEMS V8 4.6, 25 6T
years.cta: Search for 1994–2001 Range Rover P38a replacement engines on EngineMarket

years.preview: L322 Generation III Launch: Ford TD6 & Jaguar V8 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The L322 third-generation Range Rover arrived under Ford ownership and introduced two entirely new engine families - a BMW-derived 3.0 TD6 diesel and a Jaguar-sourced AJ-V8 petrol - transforming the model into a genuine luxury flagship. Key Changes Ford/BMW 3.0-litre TD6 diesel (M57) replaced the P38a's 2.5 six-cylinder, doubling torque availability Jaguar AJ-V8 4.4-litre naturally aspirated petrol replaces Rover V8, bringing a more refined luxury-class unit 4.2-litre supercharged AJ-V8 introduced for Range Rover Sport (L320) at launch in 2005 - Land Rover's first factory-supercharged engine ZF 5HP24 five-speed automatic transmission fitted across the range replacing the older four-speed unit Main Engines M57 (306D1 / TD6) - 3.0-litre six-cylinder diesel (Range Rover L322) AJ-V8 4.4 - 4.4-litre naturally aspirated petrol (Range Rover L322) AJ-V8 4.2SC - 4.2-litre supercharged petrol (Range Rover L322 Vogue SE, Range Rover Sport L320 from 2005) Popular Models Range Rover L322 3.0 TD6 Vogue Range Rover L322 4.4 V8 Vogue SE Range Rover L322 4.2 V8 Supercharged Known For TD6 M57 diesel is robust and long-lived but EGR valve fouling and swirl flap failures are common from 80,000 miles AJ-V8 4.4 offers smooth delivery but known for timing chain stretch at high mileage if servicing intervals are extended 4.2 supercharged variant generates strong enthusiast demand; supercharger nose cone seal failures are a known weak point Ford-era L322 benefits from broad specialist network and good parts availability in the UK [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: 306D1, M57, AJ-V8 4.4, AJ-V8 4.2SC, 448PN

years.preview: Range Rover Sport L320 & L322 Facelift: TDV6 & TDV8 Entry 🔴 Performance Landmark
years.description: Short intro: The launch of the Range Rover Sport L320 in 2005 introduced the 2.7-litre TDV6 diesel to the Range Rover family and brought the 3.6-litre TDV8 in 2006 - expanding the diesel lineup into genuine performance diesel territory for the first time. Key Changes 2.7-litre Ford-sourced TDV6 (276DT) introduced in Range Rover Sport L320 at launch - the first V6 diesel in the family 3.6-litre TDV8 (368DT) introduced in Range Rover Sport 2006 and Range Rover L322 from 2007, producing up to 272 bhp AJ133 5.0-litre naturally aspirated V8 introduced in L322 2005 facelift alongside the supercharged variant L322 gains ZF 6HP26 six-speed automatic from 2006, improving both refinement and fuel economy Main Engines 276DT (TDV6) - 2.7-litre V6 diesel (Range Rover Sport L320) 368DT (TDV8) - 3.6-litre V8 diesel (Range Rover Sport L320, Range Rover L322) AJ133 5.0 NA - 5.0-litre naturally aspirated V8 petrol (Range Rover L322 from 2005 facelift) AJ-V8 4.2SC - 4.2-litre supercharged V8 (Range Rover Sport L320 HSE/Autobiography) Popular Models Range Rover Sport L320 2.7 TDV6 Range Rover Sport L320 3.6 TDV8 Range Rover L322 3.6 TDV8 Vogue Range Rover Sport L320 4.2 Supercharged Known For TDV6 276DT is a proven Ford Duratorq unit - durable under normal service but injector seal leaks and EGR fouling emerge from 70,000 miles TDV8 368DT offers exceptional diesel performance but chain tensioner and guide failures are documented on pre-2010 examples Sport L320 4.2 Supercharged is a landmark performance SUV; supercharger oil seal failure causes blue smoke and premature unit wear Timing chain issues on early TDV8 units prompted investigation and revised tensioners across the fleet [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: 276DT, 368DT, AJ133 5.0, AJ-V8 4.2SC

years.preview: Facelift Era: TDV8 Evolution, SDV6 & AJ133 SC 🔴 Performance Landmark
years.description: Short intro: The 2009–2010 facelifts across L322 and L320 Sport ushered in the 5.0-litre AJ133 supercharged V8 - one of the most desirable and widely sought-after Range Rover performance engines - alongside an upgraded 3.6 TDV8 and the transitional 3.0 SDV6. Key Changes AJ133 5.0-litre supercharged V8 (508PN/508PS) introduced as new performance flagship across L322 and L320, producing 510 bhp in final tune 3.0-litre SDV6 (306DT) replaces 2.7 TDV6 in Range Rover Sport from 2011 MY, bringing Euro 5 compliance and improved torque 3.6 TDV8 revised with updated chain tensioners addressing documented failures from the pre-facelift units ZF 6HP automatic replaced by ZF 8HP eight-speed across the range, significantly improving fuel economy and shift quality Main Engines AJ133 5.0SC (508PN / 508PS) - 5.0-litre supercharged V8 petrol (Range Rover L322, Range Rover Sport L320) 306DT (SDV6) - 3.0-litre V6 diesel (Range Rover Sport L320 from 2011) 368DT (TDV8) - 3.6-litre V8 diesel (Range Rover Sport L320, continuing) 276DT (TDV6) - 2.7-litre diesel (Range Rover Sport run-out) Popular Models Range Rover L322 5.0 Supercharged Autobiography Range Rover Sport L320 5.0 Supercharged Range Rover Sport L320 3.0 SDV6 Range Rover Sport L320 3.6 TDV8 Known For AJ133 5.0SC is a precision-engineered unit delivering supercar-grade performance in a luxury SUV; supercharger drive belt and intercooler water pump are known service items SDV6 306DT brings meaningful economy improvements over the TDV8 but crankshaft bearing failure has been documented on high-mileage units - a significant cost risk TDV8 revised tensioner fix resolved the most critical chain issues; well-maintained examples now regarded as dependable long-haulers ZF 8HP transmission pairing is widely regarded as one of the finest automatic gearboxes ever fitted to a Land Rover product [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: 508PN, 508PS, 306DT, 368DT, 276DT
years.cta: Get specialist quotes for 2009–2012 Range Rover 5.0 SC and SDV6 replacement engines

years.preview: L405 & L494 Sport Launch: SDV8, TDV6 & Hybrid Introduction 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The all-aluminium fourth-generation Range Rover L405 and companion L494 Sport arrived in 2012–2013 as the most technologically ambitious Land Rover products to date, introducing the 4.4-litre SDV8, a recalibrated SDV6, and the brand's first-ever diesel hybrid drivetrain. Key Changes 4.4-litre SDV8 (448DT) introduced as flagship diesel in L405 and L494, producing 339 bhp with parallel-sequential turbocharging SDV6 (306DT) carried over but recalibrated for lighter all-aluminium body - now the most popular spec in the range Range Rover L405 Hybrid launched 2014 - Land Rover's first diesel hybrid, pairing 3.0 SDV6 with an electric motor Euro 6 emissions compliance from 2015–2016 MY introduces revised fuel injection mapping and DPF updates TDV6 (276DT) discontinued in this generation; SDV6 and SDV8 carry all diesel volumes Main Engines 448DT (SDV8) - 4.4-litre V8 diesel (Range Rover L405, Range Rover Sport L494) 306DT (SDV6) - 3.0-litre V6 diesel (Range Rover L405, Range Rover Sport L494, Range Rover Velar from 2017) AJ133 5.0SC (508PN / 508PS) - 5.0-litre supercharged V8 petrol (Range Rover L405, Range Rover Sport L494) 306PS (3.0 V6 petrol supercharged) - (Range Rover Sport L494 from 2013) Popular Models Range Rover L405 3.0 SDV6 Range Rover Sport L494 3.0 SDV6 Range Rover L405 4.4 SDV8 Range Rover Sport L494 5.0 Supercharged Range Rover Sport L494 3.0 V6 SC Known For SDV6 crankshaft bearing failure is the dominant failure mode in L405 and L494 - documented from as low as 60,000 miles; specialist advice is to check oil pressure proactively SDV8 448DT is a powerful and refined diesel but shares some bearing wear patterns with the SDV6 at high mileage AJ133 5.0SC carries over proven architecture; supercharger and water pump are the primary service targets on ageing units L405 Hybrid diesel adds complexity around battery pack conditioning and hybrid system maintenance that independent specialists are now well-equipped to handle [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: 448DT, 306DT, 508PN, 508PS, 306PS
years.cta: Compare prices for 2012–2017 Range Rover L405 and Sport L494 replacement engines from UK specialists

years.preview: Ingenium 2.0 Petrol & Diesel Arrives in Sport & Velar 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The 2017 model year brought Land Rover's in-house Ingenium four-cylinder family into the Sport and the new Velar, ending the brand's reliance on external-supplier engines in the mid-range and bringing Euro 6 compliance as standard. Key Changes Ingenium AJ20D4 2.0-litre diesel introduced in Range Rover Sport L494 and Range Rover Velar L560 - first Ingenium unit in full-size Range Rover family Ingenium AJ20P4 2.0-litre petrol launched simultaneously in Velar and Sport, replacing the 2.0 Si4 turbocharged Ford-derived unit Range Rover Evoque L538 transitions fully to Ingenium family by 2015–2016, preceding Sport and Velar Discovery Sport (L550) runs full Ingenium lineup from 2015 - confirming Ingenium as the cross-brand standard for four-cylinder P400e plug-in hybrid (PHEV) platform confirmed for next phase, previewed in this period Main Engines AJ20D4 (Ingenium 2.0 diesel) - 2.0-litre four-cylinder diesel (Range Rover Sport L494, Range Rover Velar L560, Range Rover Evoque L538, Discovery Sport L550) AJ20P4 (Ingenium 2.0 petrol) - 2.0-litre four-cylinder petrol (Range Rover Velar, Range Rover Sport, Evoque, Discovery Sport) 306DT (SDV6) - 3.0-litre diesel continues in L405 and L494 508PN / 508PS - 5.0SC continues in L405 and L494 Popular Models Range Rover Sport L494 2.0 Ingenium D240 Range Rover Velar L560 2.0 D180 / D240 Range Rover Evoque L538 2.0 eD4 / TD4 Land Rover Discovery Sport L550 2.0 Ingenium Known For Ingenium AJ20D4 delivers class-leading fuel economy but early units (pre-2018) suffer from EGR cooler cracking and coolant contamination - a serious and well-documented failure Timing chain tensioner wear on early AJ20D4 units is a known recurring theme; specialist rebuilders now offer uprated tensioner kits AJ20P4 petrol is regarded as more robust than the diesel equivalent; carbon build-up on intake ports is an evolving long-term complaint Ingenium family marks Land Rover's first genuinely in-house modern engine family - a heritage moment for the brand with real long-term parts security [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: AJ20D4, AJ20P4, 306DT, 508PN, 508PS, 204D3, 204DTA

years.preview: PHEV, Ingenium Inline-Six & P400e 🔴 Technology Introduction
years.description: Short intro: This period delivered Land Rover's first-generation plug-in hybrid across multiple Range Rover models alongside the landmark straight-six Ingenium engine - signalling a decisive shift in the brand's powertrain philosophy toward electrification. Key Changes P400e PHEV (PT204 / AJ20P4 + electric motor) launched in Range Rover L405 and Range Rover Sport L494 from 2018 - Land Rover's first production PHEV Ingenium AJ30 inline-six (3.0-litre six-cylinder MHEV) introduced in Range Rover Sport HST 2019, producing 400 PS 48V mild-hybrid (MHEV) technology introduced across AJ20D4 diesel lineup from 2019–2020 MY Second-generation Range Rover Evoque (L551) launched 2019 with full Ingenium lineup including MHEV option SDV8 448DT phased out from L405 by 2018; SDV6 becomes sole diesel for the full-size Range Rover Main Engines PT204 / AJ20P4 + EM - 2.0-litre PHEV petrol + electric (Range Rover L405 P400e, Range Rover Sport L494 P400e) AJ30 Ingenium I6 (P400 MHEV) - 3.0-litre straight-six mild hybrid (Range Rover Sport L494 HST/Autobiography from 2019) AJ20D4 MHEV - 2.0-litre diesel mild hybrid (Range Rover Evoque L551, Discovery Sport L550 from 2019) AJ20P4 MHEV - 2.0-litre petrol mild hybrid (Evoque L551, Discovery Sport from 2020) 306DT (SDV6) - 3.0-litre diesel (Range Rover L405 continues) Popular Models Range Rover L405 P400e PHEV Range Rover Sport L494 P400e PHEV Range Rover Sport L494 P400 HST Range Rover Evoque L551 2.0 D165 MHEV Land Rover Discovery Sport L550 2.0 D180 MHEV Known For P400e PHEV battery pack degradation and charge port module failures are the emerging concerns - with specialist refurbishment now available Ingenium I6 AJ30 is widely praised for its combination of refinement, torque, and efficiency; early units show strong reliability in specialist feedback 48V MHEV belt-integrated starter-generator (BISG) adds an additional maintenance item but is generally regarded as a reliable system in normal UK use EGR cooler issues from the AJ20D4 carry forward into MHEV variants - owners of 2019–2020 diesel MHEV models should proactively inspect cooler condition [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: PT204, AJ20P4, AJ30P6, AJ20D4 MHEV, 306DT, 448DT (run-out)

years.preview: Generation V (L460) & L461 Evoque: Full Ingenium, PHEV Standard & V8 Return 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The fifth-generation Range Rover L460 launched in 2022 with a modernised Ingenium lineup as standard fit, PHEV across all primary grades, and the reintroduction of a BMW-sourced V8 petrol - marking both a technological leap and a deliberate nod to the brand's luxury heritage. Key Changes All-new L460 body-on-aluminium platform introduced with Ingenium AJ30 3.0-litre inline-six as the core engine across all trim levels P530 4.4-litre twin-turbocharged BMW V8 petrol (N63 / B44) introduced in Range Rover L460 SV - the first V8 since the AJ133 era P460e and P510e plug-in hybrids offered as standard-fit options across the L460 range, not as limited derivatives Range Rover Velar updated with full Ingenium lineup; Evoque L551 continues MHEV programme Electric Range Rover (Electric) confirmed for 2025 release - first all-electric Range Rover Main Engines AJ30 I6 MHEV (P360 / P400) - 3.0-litre mild-hybrid inline-six petrol (Range Rover L460, Range Rover Sport L461) D300 I6 MHEV - 3.0-litre mild-hybrid inline-six diesel (Range Rover L460, Range Rover Sport L461) P460e / P510e PHEV - 3.0-litre inline-six + electric (Range Rover L460) BMW N63/B44 4.4 V8 TT - 4.4-litre twin-turbo V8 petrol (Range Rover L460 SV P530) AJ20D4 / AJ20P4 MHEV - 2.0-litre four-cylinder (Range Rover Evoque L551, Discovery Sport L550, Velar continued) Popular Models Range Rover L460 3.0 D300 MHEV Range Rover L460 P400 MHEV Range Rover L460 P460e PHEV Range Rover L460 SV P530 V8 Range Rover Sport L461 P360 MHEV Range Rover Sport L461 P400 MHEV Known For AJ30 I6 MHEV is the refinement benchmark for the current Range Rover range; inherited BMW-side oil consumption tendencies are a known long-term watch point PHEV models deliver strong electric-only range (circa 60 miles) in real-world UK use; high-voltage battery pack longevity remains the key question for future replacement demand L461 Sport shares drivetrain completely with L460 - owners of both models benefit from identical engine parts compatibility and shared specialist knowledge [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: AJ30P6, AJ30D6, AJ20D4, AJ20P4, BMW N63, B44, PT204

faq.h2: Range Rover Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: Quick, honest answers to the most common questions Range Rover owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a Range Rover engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A replacement Range Rover engine typically costs between £1,800 for a used 2.2-litre Evoque unit and £9,500 for a fully reconditioned 5.0 V8 supercharged with upgraded timing components. Reconditioned 3.0 SDV6 diesels – the most commonly replaced engine – range from £3,500 to £6,500 supply & fit. Used engines start around £800 for older units but carry higher repeat failure risk. Fitting adds £500–£1,500 depending on model and specialist location. Compare three quotes minimum – prices on the same engine code can vary by over £1,000. → Compare Range Rover engine replacement prices now
items.cta: Compare Range Rover engine prices now

items.question: What affects the cost of a Range Rover engine replacement?
items.answer: Four factors drive price: engine size and rarity (5.0 V8 costs more than 2.0 diesel), condition (reconditioned with upgrades costs more than used), vehicle complexity (L405 takes longer than L322), and supply-only versus supply & fit. Location also matters – London specialists typically charge 20–30% more than Midlands or Northern England workshops. EngineMarket's quote tool captures all these variables so you compare like-for-like. Always ask whether the quoted price includes fitting, delivery, and VAT. → Get quotes from UK Range Rover engine specialists
items.cta: Compare Range Rover engine prices now

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my Range Rover?
items.answer: In most cases, yes – especially if the chassis, gearbox, and body are solid. A reconditioned SDV6 with uprated bearings costs £4,000–£6,500 fitted, compared to spending £15,000–£30,000 on a replacement vehicle of similar age and specification. Rebuilt engines with upgraded components often outlast the original factory units because they address the known design flaw. For high-value models like L405 Autobiography or L494 SVR, replacement is almost always financially sensible. For very high-mileage L320 Sports worth under £6,000, a good used engine may be the smarter short-term option. → Find reconditioned Range Rover engines with comprehensive warranty cover
items.cta: Get a Range Rover engine quote now

items.question: What's included in a Range Rover engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: A full supply & fit package includes: sourcing a reconditioned or used engine matched to your VIN, removing the failed unit, fitting the replacement with new gaskets, seals, fluids, and filters, proper disposal of the old engine, and a road test to verify operation. Most specialists include a 12-month workmanship warranty on fitting alongside the engine's parts warranty. Supply-only costs £500–£1,500 less but leaves you to arrange fitting locally. Always confirm whether ancillaries like turbochargers, injectors, and the oil pump are transferred or replaced. → Compare Range Rover engine supply and fit deals
items.cta: Get a Range Rover engine quote now

items.question: How long does a Range Rover engine replacement take?
items.answer: For common Range Rover models like the L322, L405, L494, or L320 Sport with readily available engine codes (3.0 SDV6, 4.4 TDV8, 5.0 V8), a like-for-like swap takes 3–5 working days. More complex models, hybrid variants, or rare engine codes can take 5–7 days. The biggest variable is engine availability – our network of 150+ UK specialists stocks the most commonly replaced units, but niche or older engines may add 2–3 days for delivery. Ask your supplier for a confirmed timeline before committing. → Compare Range Rover engine lead times and prices side by side
items.cta: Get a Range Rover engine quote now

items.question: Do reconditioned Range Rover engines come with warranty?
items.answer: Yes – every engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty. Reconditioned and remanufactured units from premium specialists often include extended 24-month coverage. The warranty typically covers major internal components: crankshaft, bearings, pistons, timing chain, oil pump, and cylinder head. Wear items like belts, gaskets, and filters are usually covered under the fitting warranty (labour) not the parts warranty. Extended warranties beyond 24 months are sometimes available on rebuilt units with upgraded components. Always confirm exact terms – including labour coverage – with your chosen supplier before purchase. → Get warranty-secured Range Rover engine quotes now

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned Range Rover engine?
items.answer: Used engines cost £800–£4,000 and work well as a short-term fix – ideal if you plan to sell the vehicle within 12 months or have a very tight budget. However, used SDV6 or TDV6 units carry the same crankshaft risk as your failed engine. Reconditioned engines cost £2,800–£9,500 but include upgraded components (uprated bearings, reinforced chains, revised oil pumps), full bench testing, and a 12–24 month warranty. If you plan to keep your Range Rover for 3+ years or cover high annual mileage, reconditioned with upgrades is almost always better value long-term. → Use our free comparison tool to see whether a rebuilt or used Range Rover engine makes financial sense

items.question: What are the most common Range Rover engine failures?
items.answer: The most common catastrophic failures driving replacement: 3.0 SDV6/TDV6 crankshaft bearing fatigue (snapped crank, L405, L494, L320), 5.0 V8 timing chain tensioner wear (L322, L405, Sport), 4.4 TDV8 crankshaft fracture and turbo actuator failure, 2.0 Ingenium timing chain stretch (Evoque, Velar), and 2.7 TDV6 plastic oil pump housing fracture (Sport L320). Early detection rarely prevents these failures – they typically cause sudden, irreversible engine death. Replacement with a reconditioned unit containing upgraded components is the only permanent solution. → Compare replacement prices for common Range Rover problem engines

items.question: How can I get the best price on a Range Rover engine?
items.answer: Compare at least three quotes – prices vary by £1,000+ on identical engine codes across different specialists. Supply-only saves £500–£1,500 if you have a trusted local garage. Always ask: is delivery included? Is VAT included? Is the 12-month warranty parts-only or parts-and-labour?
